sentences.text: I don't remember exactly her name.
I believe it was [INAUDIBLE].
I could be wrong.
I really don't remember, but I think that was her name.

And my one experience was when she told me to rest that the kommandant from the [GERMAN], which was the concentration part-- camp part of Theresienstadt not the ghetto part, was riding by with his horse.
And I had the feeling that he saw me, so I had never, ever been afraid like that in my life.
But I got away.
[LAUGHS]
Well, I would have been dead immediately.
I think he would have shot me, or I would have landed up right in the concentration camp there and that would have been it.
Because nobody came out from [GERMAN] anymore.
[SIGHS] I think I was working there from '40-- the end of '43, something like that-- middle of '43-- I really don't remember-- till the-- till we were sent to Auschwitz.
Yeah, there was a Judenrat.
There was a Judenrat.
We had a few different ones.
I remember Dr.
Edelstain who was one, and Mamelstain.
And one was from Czechoslovakia, and one was from Vienna.
And then there also was one from Germany.
Now, I just know that Edelstain was the one from Brunn, and the other one was from [INAUDIBLE] from Czechoslovakia there, and one was also from Germany.
But I don't know which ones they were.
Their function was to elect people, number one, to go to the east into-- to Auschwitz when transports went.
To hold the ghetto for the Germans together to go after the German laws.
And it wasn't an easy target-- task.
And I know two of 'em didn't make it out.
I think one was liberated in Theresienstadt.
I don't know which one it was, but I know Dr.
Edelstain went to Auschwitz with a family transport before I did.
With the family, and that was a-- I think [? succor ?] was name also.
He [? was ?] something in the, you know, in a Judenrat or whatever they called it.
And--
They were appointed from the Germans.
I have no idea.
I really have no idea how they did it.
Well, of course it was a good position, because you had your own rooms, and you had your-- a different type of a ghetto life then we had.
It was an easier life, but they went earlier than us to Auschwitz and none of them came back.
They were killed-- first ones.
They called [INAUDIBLE] they were called the [GERMAN], prominent to the other people.
And there were transports going in 1943 and 1944, and 1944 mainly, to Auschwitz with only prominent people on there.
And it wasn't only from the Judenrat.
There were other prominents also, you know.
They had a Jewish police.
It was called a ghetto [? worker, ?] and they were just men and women.
A few of them were women, I believe.
No.
I don't know if there were women.
I really-- I really don't-- I think there were few women in the ghetto [? worker ?] too, and the men.
But they were just regular Jewish citizens who came from all over like we did, and then were appointed to work there as ghetto [? worker. ?]
That also was considered a better position, but most of them didn't make it anyway, so--
Their function was that-- we had, for instance, a curfew at night, a certain time every-- the whole ghetto had to be inside.
Nobody could be on the streets and et cetera, so they had to make sure that nobody was on the streets.
If [? they ?] caught somebody, they would be arrested form the ghetto [? worker, ?] and then the SS would take over, et cetera.
They didn't have an easy task either, because it was like a Jew against Jew.
And they really, really tried-- most of 'em-- tried to be good, but it wasn't easy.
From the ghetto [? worker, ?] no.
Not really.
No, I really-- personally, I didn't really know anybody, you know, by name [INAUDIBLE].
Yes.
Yes, it was all surrounded with the-- a lot of uniformed SS with guns.
Wherever you went, there-- you know, you run into somebody in uniform.
And especially when we went out of the ghetto.
And this was our life there.
I know that there was a resistance in Theresienstadt.
There were two resistance--
--groups.
One was the-- called-- was Shalomo-- Shalom, Shalomo, which was a tiny part of the resistance.
And one was the Czeco, and that was for Czech Communists, the Left organization.
And I personally knew some people who were in there, but I don't like to mention the name.
One of them, I know, is in New York, a friend of ours, but I don't want to mention their name.

I spoke to him the other day to find a little more about-- out about it, and he is going in November to Theresien by invitation from the Czech government and the ghetto organizations there still in the museum.
And he's going to give a lecture from the real inside of the resistance, which a lot of people don't even know about that it was going on.
So they had people going out from the ghetto, and there was a time that in-- in Czechoslovakia in some of the villages there were Jewish children hidden.
They still bought them to-- from-- even some of the children, I believe, out of the ghetto to Yugoslavia, and from Yugoslavia to Italy, and tried to get them out.
And there was a resistance there, which really had hoped at one time that it could be like what happened in the Warsaw ghetto with better success, but nothing happened.
Went too fast.
Did they have-- did they have weapons?
That I don't know.
I really-- I can't say.
I don't know.
I surmise yes, but I don't know.
The Shalomo.
Yes.
Yes, I did.
I don't want to talk about it, because I understand it was a very secret thing.
And up to now when people found out-- I mean, that they came out with it that it really existed.
Most of the people who were in Theresienstadt and were liberated don't even believe it, because for the simple reason they didn't know about it.
It was very, very secretive.
And it really only came out like the last few years.
And I also know some people who were in the Czeco.
It was, a matter of fact, a very close friend of mine, and he made it out.
He came to United States, and he died here a few years ago.
So I'd rather don't mention names or anything.
I think--
You know what I believe that they going to publicize it after November 1996.
The whole thing, what really was going on, because this is what I understand the meetings are for now.
I think it's on the 15th of November in Czechoslovakia.
Yes.
I understand a few children, they got out.
They still got them to Palestine at that time, one way or the other, and they came back.
And I know about one person.
I don't know if you heard about [INAUDIBLE]?
Him I can mention for the simple reason that he's from Vienna, and he was very, very well known in the Zionist organizations.
And he was called-- what they call in [INAUDIBLE] I think, [NON-ENGLISH].
You ever heard that word?
It's somebody who does for children and et cetera, and he was one who was also from Vienna, bringing children back and forth, and bringing children to Palestine, and came back and returned.
And he also came to the ghetto, and went even, I believe, out of the ghetto and then returned, and then was sent to Auschwitz.
So there was a lot of things which a-- a lot of people don't know about.
But there is a book out on him, so his name I can mention.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah, I know him, too.
Yes, I did know him.
I belonged to a Zionist organization, but at that time I didn't know it had anything to do with resistance [? or so. ?] I mean, we were children.
We made our own organizations, and we gave us names.
And mine was the [?
Habonim. ?] [?
Habonim. ?] Yeah.
This was while we were in the ghetto.
Yeah.
In the rooms where we stayed.
You know, like us girls, and-- and from the other rooms, the boys.
We would come in, and we-- we learned Hebrew.
We learned the songs.
We learned history.
So any knowledge I have, I got it from over there secretly.
[LAUGHS] Yeah.
We thought it was active.
We thought-- I don't know how active we were, and the [INAUDIBLE], they were older than us, so they most likely somehow were more involved with everything than we were, what we didn't know about.
But they prepared us that if we would be liberated to become really Zionists.
Oh, yes.
Oh, are you kidding?
That was my dream to go.
But then my mother and I came back together.
My mother was very sick.
I couldn't have gotten her out.
And we came to the state-- sorry.
I was told not to play with that.
[LAUGHS]
In the ghetto there is so much.
I don't know where to start or where end really.
Let's leave it at this.
I saw other people mistreated, but I wasn't.
I saw older people mistreated in the streets.
That the Germans went through, and when they couldn't see the Jewish star-- even in ghetto you had to wear it-- you know, they would hit them and-- and all kinds of different things.
I mean, it wasn't mistreated already that people were hungry and didn't have enough food.
The ghetto was what we called a [GERMAN].
At one time, there were thousands of people, and other times there were less people, because the transport went and then it came, you know, back and forth.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
It was very, very crowded.
Very, very crowded.
A lot. ?]
I personally don't know.
We stole food from the Germans, let's put it that way.
But people-- I don't know when they're hungry if they take things.
You never know.
I mean, we were children, and we got out and we did things [? still. ?] We had a way of getting things.
But to try to get things--
I don't know.
I really don't know.
Apartments you couldn't get.
You just were in the room where you were.
And more food, I don't know.
I don't know.
It was all at the same time.
I don't know how they were connected, because I know the Zionist group and I know the Communist group were not really the best of friends at one time.
But then I guess in the ghetto, they connected.
They must've been in the existence already in Prague and in Brunn.
I think they did.
I don't know personally, but I think they did from Theresienstadt.
I think they did.
I think that was in the making.
It was in the making of, but never got to it.
Because there were too many transport going already.
Couldn't go ahead.
Too many young people left, and the oldest-- oldest ones were behind.
The old ones were left behind.
That's what it is.
No.
No.
Like I told you, we had that wall, which was normally [INTERPOSING VOICES] built around [? garrison ?] town.
I don't remember.
I really don't remember.
[SIGHS] I'm not sure, but I think it was like about 7:00, [BACKROUND NOISE] 8:00 o'clock in the evening, something on that order.
I'm not sure.
And then they also had a-- a countdown every-- wherever you lived.
What room you were in, or in a home, or in a building, or-- or wherever people lived together, there was a countdown every day.
They knew there were so and so many people in that building, and there had to be so and so many people in that building, you know?
So-- Someone would come around to check how many people?
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
That I know.
Yes.
Yes, there were marriages in the ghetto.
What they called ghetto weddings.
People got together and they got married.
Most of 'em went individually or together to Auschwitz.
A few of 'em survived, a few didn't.
I know of one who was together with me.
She got married in Theresienstadt, a girl from Brunn.
And she went to Auschwitz with me and her husband also.
I saw them again in Israel.
They live in Israel now, and--
She was-- her maiden name was Ticho-Tichova.
Lilly Tichova and he was the-- Herbert Schwartzwald.
And they changed their name in Israel to-- no, not in Israel.
In Czechoslovakia after the war when they met again, they changed their name to Sabbotka, and they go under the name Sabbotka in Israel.
And-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]
Pardon me?
Yeah, there were children born-- there were children born in ghetto.
A friend of mine, one of the doctors and his wife, had a baby in ghetto.
And they went to Auschwitz and didn't survive.
[INAUDIBLE] Dr.
Schwartz-- no, not Dr.
Schwartz.
It was Dr.
Froid.
Dr.
Walter Froid.
He was a Czech doctor with his wife, who took care of the kids in the home where I was in.
Not in the hospital.
And he had a baby and went to Auschwitz together.
[SIGHS] Let's see.
I left in 1942.
I was 15.
Yeah.
I had a boyfriend in Theresienstadt, too.
A Czech boy from Brunn, and--
His name was Kurt Karnel, and he went to Auschwitz to transport before me.
His mother and his sister went with me to Auschwitz, but didn't survive.
Actually, they survived the selection.
They were with us in the same barracks, but they didn't make it out.
I have never seen him again.
After the war, he-- I don't know-- I was still in Germany.
I found-- no, I wasn't in Germany.
I was here already in 1947.
I met somebody who knew him and knew him and me, and he told me that he's living in Prague.
So we got in touch with each other, and we were writing for a while.
But by then, I was married already.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we wrote for a while together.
He had married and had a little boy.
And tried to get out of Czechoslovakia that time, which was very, very hard.

And then later on I found out-- matter of fact, through that friend who was in the resistance, who is here in New York-- that he came to the United States and lived here in New York, in Brooklyn somewhere.
And he died about two, three years ago-- four years ago, or something like that.
But I never saw him again.
We never got in contact with each other, you know?
One of those things.
Where there are Jewish prayers in the ghetto?
Were-- were holidays celebrated?
Oh, yes.
Yes.
They were the rabbis from-- who were in Theresienstadt from different towns.
I don't know if it was allowed or not, but it was done.

And I always [? feel ?] whatever we did included the lessons they gave us, you know, like schooling and things like that-- I always had the feeling that the SS knew, but they also knew we wouldn't make it there.
That we would be sent out anyway, the younger [INAUDIBLE].
Now with the rabbis, I know that they had services, you know, in different places, et cetera.
Well, [SIGHS] that's in question.
I saw a nun in Theresienstadt.
I don't know who she was and how she got there.
I don't know what nationality she was, but I saw her.
I don't know her.
No, I didn't-- I didn't-- I saw her, but I didn't know her.
I saw her in street.
We had a girl who was-- her name was Dolly.
I forgot her last name.
She was from the mountains from Austria.
She was a couple of years older than us girls, and we were like 16 and 17, and she was already 19, 20 or more.
And also the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] her father's name.
No, she only knew her mother.
She was an illegitimate child.
And it turned out that her father was a Jew from Vienna.
They arrested her, and she ended up in Theresienstadt.
And she went to Auschwitz and didn't come back.
As a matter of fact, my sister-in-law and I always talking about her, because we were friends with her.
And we must have been the first two-- I mean, not only us personally-- but when she got to Theresienstadt, we must have been the first Jews she ever saw.
She didn't even know anything.
So people ended up like-- like that too, you know?
But I remember seeing the nun with the big cross, walking around in her habit or whatever you call it.
So she must've been arrested somewhere.
And I don't think she was Jewish.
I mean, that--
Not in Theresienstadt.
We had-- I was in a gypsy camp in Auschwitz [INAUDIBLE].
I shouldn't really say that, because I know somebody who is here in New York, and his mother was a Jewish woman from [INAUDIBLE] or [INAUDIBLE], and his father was a gypsy.
And he and his mother and his sister came to Theresienstadt, and the sister-- the mother survived and he survived, and his sister didn't come back from Auschwitz.
Well, these people in particular went as Jews, right?
But in-- in Auschwitz, I don't remember.
I really don't.
I think [? that ?] we-- we all looked alike, no matter what.
No.
The only good-- the only thing is with all the bad things and everything, I still had my mother and father together, you know?
It's like a home away from home.
[BACKROUND NOISE] Still-- still was there I saw my mother.
I saw my father, you know?
So other than that, we had a tough time.
We made the best out of it.
And when I read about it, like the Germans claim that the Theresienstadt was a [GERMAN] ghetto, right?
Like a special type of treatments.
You know what made it?
The people in there, especially the younger people.
Because everybody tried to keep their dignity, no matter what.
They still tried to learn.
They still tried-- they didn't know what was ahead.
We all didn't know what was still ahead of us, which in a way was good that we didn't know.
Oh, yes.
Oh, we--
All what we were living for to get out and go to Israel.
I mean, to Palestine at the time.
That was our dream.
So.
Yeah, the Red Cross was there, and the Red Cross from Sweden, I think.
It was ?] the Swedish Red Cross was there.
And they fixed up the ghetto ahead of time.
They were expecting company.
And they had--
Oh, they cleaned two, three streets over there in one particular center, and it was very close where the youth organization-- I mean, the youth homes were.
And they taught us in the [GERMAN].
They actually trained us for days for that.
They put food and stuff into baskets, and we had to march in the ghetto-- into the ghetto and were filmed in ghetto from-- what was his name?
A-- a Jewish film [INAUDIBLE] who came from Berlin.
His name was [?
Gavon, ?] and he took the films from Theresienstadt.
He didn't make [INAUDIBLE].
After that he went to Auschwitz.
This is right after that every-- all the transports started.
Were you allowed to eat that food?
We were allowed to eat that food, and [INAUDIBLE], you know, they-- they taught us what to say.
They said, oh, don't we have anything else to eat?
We always have to eat the same thing.
Don't we have something this time?
Couldn't you bring in, let's say, for instance, strawberries instead of apples, you know?
I don't know exactly the words anymore more, but that's what they taught us.
Didn't help much for the one day, two days.
No, I just remember that we walked in there with the things in there, and they took that film.
No, we had usually in the summer times, we had like a-- we made from-- mostly from bedsheets and stuff, we made our own clothes.
And some people who could sew.
We had like slacks and shorts.
And in the winter time, I don't know.
We-- we had very little clothes, but we made the best out of it.
That was still the ghetto life.
But when they were trying to fix the place up to make everything look very nice--
Yeah.
Oh, they made like a children pavilion where the kids could play and all kinds of things.
No, no.
No, no.
Nothing like that.
Not that I remember.
Most of-- most of us had short hair anyway, because of lice and et cetera in the beginning there.
Yes, they cleaned the street.
They had a-- what did they call it?
They [GERMAN].
They were people who had to clean the streets up and fixed up some of the buildings from the outside.
Just for the Red Cross.
Yeah, they had a-- a ghetto music.
They had a ghetto concert-- a-- a ghetto band play music.
And-- and I think, I believe at that time they had what they called a cafe house.
They made like-- people could sit outside in a park area, you know, in a plaza [BACKROUND NOISE] type of thing.
But this was like nothing.
It was a joke.
Everything was gone.
Everything was gone again.
Everything was gone, was the same.
Right after the thing when the Red Cross came and did the filming, the transport started again.
Eichmann came.
I saw Eichmann many times in-- in Theresienstadt.
And as soon Eichmann came, you knew there was a transport going, or more then that--
You know how I remember him?
Short, not tall.
A short man in uniform.
I understand that his family lived at one time as missionaries in Palestine.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And what happened is that he approached some of the people and spoke Hebrew to them, because he lived in Palestine as a child or as a young man.
But he was known for what he was.
Everybody was-- knew when he came, there was something was going on.
OK.
